text.title: Portfolio Strategy (70/20/10) Core Rules
text.summary: Three-horizon investment allocation (70% Core H1, 20% Adjacent H2, 10% Transformational H3) for a PM owning multiple shipped products; rebalances quarterly with explicit kill triggers.

rule.statement: Portfolio investment MUST be partitioned by horizon: 70% H1 (Core, 0-12mo), 20% H2 (Adjacent, 12-24mo), 10% H3 (Transformational, 24-36mo). Drift beyond ±5pp triggers a rebalance.
rule.rationale: Without explicit horizons, all capacity bleeds into H1 firefighting; H3 starves.

rule.statement: Each product MUST be tagged H1/H2/H3 with rationale. Untagged products default to H1 (forcing the conversation).
rule.rationale: Untagged products distort the allocation calc; default-H1 forces the operator to confirm or re-classify.

rule.statement: Each horizon has its own kill trigger: H1 = sustained activation/retention slip, H2 = 18-month no-PMF, H3 = 36-month no-market-signal.
rule.rationale: Without horizon-appropriate triggers, the team kills H3 too fast (no signal yet) or keeps H1 too long (sunk cost).

rule.statement: The portfolio mix MUST be reviewed quarterly with a written rebalance memo. Skipping the review allows silent drift.
rule.rationale: Annual-only reviews lock in stale allocations; quarterly reviews catch drift while it is cheap to fix.

rule.statement: Portfolio strategy decisions (allocation, kill, expand) are owned at portfolio-PM level; per-product roadmap stays with product PMs.
rule.rationale: Role confusion produces parallel decision-making; clear role line keeps both levels effective.

rule.statement: If applicability conditions fail (any Skip-If trigger holds — single-product team; pre-PMF; agency/services; recent memo without trigger events), the agent MUST skip this methodology and route to the named alternative or to the methodology fallback.
rule.rationale: Mis-applying a methodology outside its preconditions degrades output quality and pollutes downstream artefacts; the explicit skip-rule keeps the routing tree honest.
